combat_history.event: Moskva operated in the Black Sea during the conflict, providing naval support and establishing sea control. No direct combat engagement reported.
combat_history.significance: First major deployment since Cold War, demonstrated continued operational capability

combat_history.event: Moskva and Varyag rotated through Mediterranean deployments supporting Russian operations in Syria, providing air defense umbrella for naval task force.
combat_history.significance: Showed ability to project power and provide area air defense in contested environment

combat_history.event: Moskva was struck by two Ukrainian Neptune anti-ship missiles while operating south of Odesa. Ship suffered catastrophic damage and sank while under tow, with significant crew casualties.
combat_history.significance: First combat loss of a major Russian warship since WWII, demonstrated vulnerability to modern anti-ship missiles and poor damage control capabilities

modernization.description: Comprehensive upgrade including new combat systems, improved radar and electronics, Kalibr cruise missile capability via P-1000 tubes
modernization.impact: Added land-attack capability and improved sensors, extending service life

modernization.description: Similar upgrade to Marshal Ustinov planned but cancelled due to budget constraints and Ukraine conflict
modernization.impact: Varyag remains in original 1980s configuration with limited modernization

vulnerabilities.description: Soviet-era damage control philosophy and systems proved inadequate during Moskva sinking. Limited compartmentalization and firefighting capability.
vulnerabilities.context: Modern anti-ship missiles can cause catastrophic fires that Soviet-era systems cannot contain
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Some improvements made during modernizations but fundamental design limitations remain

vulnerabilities.description: Aging radar and electronic systems vulnerable to modern jamming and electronic attack. Limited ECCM capability on non-modernized hulls.
vulnerabilities.context: NATO electronic warfare capabilities have advanced significantly since 1980s design
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Modernized ships receive improved EW suites but coverage is limited

vulnerabilities.description: Design optimized for anti-ship warfare with limited flexibility for other missions. Poor ASW capability and limited land-attack options (except modernized hulls).
vulnerabilities.context: Modern naval warfare requires multi-mission platforms
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Kalibr integration provides some land-attack capability but fundamental limitations remain

vulnerabilities.description: Complex propulsion system requires extensive maintenance. Ukrainian shipyard closure eliminated major refit capability.
vulnerabilities.context: Russian naval maintenance capabilities are stretched and lacking advanced facilities
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Limited options for major overhauls, likely to impact long-term availability

operational_patterns.typical_deployment: Flagship of surface action groups, Mediterranean rotational deployments, fleet exercises
operational_patterns.typical_task_group: Operates with destroyer and frigate escorts, submarine support, auxiliary vessels
operational_patterns.readiness_notes: Availability limited by maintenance requirements and crew training levels. Only 1-2 ships typically operationally available.

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Ticonderoga emphasizes air defense and multi-mission capability with Aegis system and VLS flexibility, while Slava focuses on pure anti-ship firepower with massive P-1000 missiles. Ticonderoga has superior electronics and damage control but less anti-ship punch.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Cold War philosophy clash - concentrated firepower vs. multi-mission flexibility

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 055 represents evolution of Soviet heavy destroyer concept with modern sensors and VLS flexibility. Similar displacement but vastly superior electronics and multi-mission capability. Shows how Soviet concepts evolved with modern technology.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: How China perfected the Soviet heavy warship concept

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Kirov is larger and more heavily armed but shares similar Soviet design philosophy. Nuclear propulsion gives unlimited range. Both designed for same mission but Kirov represents 'maximum' approach while Slava is more economical.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Soviet naval gigantism - when bigger was always better

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 45 represents modern European approach - sophisticated air defense with SAMPSON radar but limited anti-ship capability. Roughly half the displacement but arguably more capable in modern threat environment.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Specialization vs. brute force - modern destroyer design philosophy
